intro.kicker: Topic 7 of 38 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: The angle of repose (α) is the maximum angle of an inclined plane with the horizontal such that a body placed on it just begins to slide. Resolving forces along and perpendicular to the incline: μs = tan α. Since angle of friction also gives tan θ = μs, the two angles are equal: α = θ. NEET tests the definition, the derivation μs = tan α, and the equality with angle of friction.

expectedQuestions.description: 1 MCQ per paper on angle of repose. NEET tests: (i) definition of angle of repose; (ii) the formula μs = tan α; (iii) the equality α = θ (angle of repose = angle of friction). Numerical substitution questions using tan⁻¹(μs) = α are common.

timeRequired.description: 25 min: 10 min definition + derivation on inclined plane, 15 min linking to angle of friction and MCQ drilling. Draw the free-body diagram for a block on an incline at angle α: mg sin α = F_l = μs mg cos α ⇒ tan α = μs.

difficulty.description: Straightforward inclined-plane force balance. The main insight is the equality α = θ (angle of repose = angle of friction). Once the force balance derivation is seen once, both formulas are immediate. Common error: using kinetic friction instead of limiting (static) friction.

nriUsaGap.description: AP Physics introduces inclined plane friction but does not formally define ‘angle of repose’ as a named quantity. NEET uses this as an explicit definition MCQ. Students from US curricula must learn the term, its definition, and the formula tan α = μs, plus the equality with angle of friction.

weightage.sectionTitle: Angle of Repose — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Definition-based MCQs: ‘Angle of repose is the angle of inclined plane at which a body just begins to slide.’
Formula tan α = μs combined with angle of friction equality is high yield.
Numerical questions: given μs, find α = tan⁻¹(μs).

strategySteps.title: Derive μs = tan α from Inclined Plane FBD
strategySteps.description: Block of mass m on incline at angle α. At the point of just beginning to slide: mg sin α = F_l (limiting friction up the incline) and R = mg cos α (normal reaction). Since F_l = μs R: mg sin α = μs mg cos α. Dividing: tan α = μs.

strategySteps.title: Prove α = θ (Angle of Repose = Angle of Friction)
strategySteps.description: From angle of friction: tan θ = μs. From angle of repose: tan α = μs. Since both equal μs, we have α = θ. This equality is explicitly tested in NEET: ‘what is the relation between angle of friction and angle of repose?’

strategySteps.title: Numerical Applications
strategySteps.description: Given μs, compute angle of repose: α = tan⁻¹(μs). Given angle α, compute μs = tan α. These are 1-step numerical MCQs. Know exact values: tan 30° = 1/√3 ≈ 0.577; tan 45° = 1; tan 60° = √3 ≈ 1.73.

cards.title: Angle of Repose Complete Notes
cards.description: Definition, FBD derivation on inclined plane, μs = tan α, equality with angle of friction, and 6 solved NEET MCQs with numerical substitution.

cards.title: Angle of Repose Quick Reference
cards.description: One-page: FBD diagram of block on incline, force labels, μs = tan α derivation, and α = θ comparison table.

subtopics.name: Definition and Relation to Angle of Friction

cards.title: Angle of Repose: Definition and Derivation
cards.coreRevision: Angle of repose (α) is the maximum angle of inclined plane with horizontal such that a body placed on it just begins to slide. FBD derivation: mg sin α = μs mg cos α ⇒ tan α = μs. Hence the coefficient of limiting friction equals the tangent of angle of repose.
points: Definition: angle at which body ‘just begins to slide’ (onset of motion, not during motion)
Use limiting (maximum static) friction in derivation, not kinetic friction
tan α = μs (same formula as angle of friction tan θ = μs)
Therefore: α = θ (angle of repose equals angle of friction)
Greater coefficient μs → larger angle of repose (steeper slope before sliding)
cards.example: μs = 0.5 ⇒ angle of repose = tan⁻¹(0.5) ≈ 26.6°. A block on a 30° slope with μs = 0.577 will just begin to slide at 30° (since tan 30° = 0.577 = μs).

cards.title: Relation to Angle of Friction: α = θ
cards.coreRevision: Angle of repose α = angle of friction θ, because both obey tan = μs. This equality is the bridge between two different physical setups (inclined plane vs flat surface) and appears as a direct NEET MCQ comparison question.
points: Angle of friction θ: measured from normal reaction in flat-surface resultant vector triangle
Angle of repose α: measured from horizontal in inclined plane geometry
Both give tan = μs ⇒ α = θ (numerically equal)
NEET MCQ: ‘angle of repose is equal to the angle of friction’ → TRUE
This equality holds only when static (limiting) friction is used, not kinetic
cards.example: If angle of friction = 37° (μs = tan 37° ≈ 0.75), then angle of repose = 37°. A slope steeper than 37° will cause the block to slide for this surface pair.

curriculum.hintText: AP Physics vs NEET on angle of repose.

gaps.title: ‘Angle of Repose’ Not a Named Concept in AP Physics
gaps.description: AP Physics covers inclined plane friction (resolving mg cosθ and mg sinθ) but does not define the ‘angle of repose’ as a formal named quantity. NEET tests it as both a definition MCQ and a formula MCQ.
bullets: Memorise: angle of repose = angle at which body just begins to slide on inclined plane.
Formula: μs = tan (angle of repose). This must be derived by FBD on incline.

gaps.title: AP Physics Does Not Teach α = θ Equality
gaps.description: The equality between angle of repose and angle of friction (α = θ) is not in AP syllabi but is explicitly in NEET. This is a straightforward corollary: both angles have tan = μs; therefore they are equal.
bullets: In NEET MCQs: ‘which of the following is equal to the angle of friction?’ → angle of repose.
Both tan α and tan θ equal μs, so α = θ can be stated directly.

questions.question: A block rests on a rough inclined plane. The angle of the incline is slowly increased. The block begins to slide when the angle reaches 37°. The angle of friction is:
questions.explanation: The angle at which the block just begins to slide is the angle of repose α = 37°. Since angle of repose = angle of friction (α = θ), the angle of friction is also 37°. The coefficient of static friction μs = tan 37° ≈ 0.75.

questions.question: Angle of repose is defined as the angle of the inclined plane with horizontal such that a body placed on it:

options.text: does not slide at any velocity

questions.explanation: By definition, angle of repose is the angle of the inclined plane with the horizontal such that a body placed on it just begins to slide. At exactly this angle, applied force (mg sin α) equals limiting friction (μs mg cos α), so the body is on the verge of motion.

questions.question: If coefficient of static friction μs = 1/√3, the angle of repose is:
Angle of repose (α) and angle of friction (θ) are related by:
On a rough inclined plane, a block just begins to slide at angle 45°. The coefficient of static friction between block and plane is:
Coefficient of limiting friction is equal to tangent of angle of repose. This statement is:

items.title: State the definition of angle of repose
items.description: Angle of the inclined plane with the horizontal such that a body placed on it just begins to slide. The word ‘just’ means onset of motion — limiting friction is used, not kinetic.

items.title: Derive tan α = μs from FBD on inclined plane
items.description: mg sin α = F_l = μs mg cos α ⇒ tan α = μs. Practice the vector decomposition once so the algebra is fast under exam conditions.

items.title: State and justify the equality α = θ
items.description: tan α = μs (from repose) and tan θ = μs (from friction angle). Both are equal to μs, so α = θ. This is the most frequently tested relation in this pair of topics.

items.title: Numerical fluency: given μs, find α
items.description: α = tan⁻¹(μs). Know: tan 30° = 1/√3, tan 37° ≈ 0.75, tan 45° = 1, tan 53° ≈ 1.33, tan 60° = √3. These cover all NEET angle-of-repose numerical MCQs.

items.question: Why is limiting (static) friction used in the derivation, not kinetic friction?
items.answer: Angle of repose is the angle at which the body ‘just begins’ to slide — the onset of motion. At this instant, the friction force is at its maximum static value (limiting friction F_l = μs R). Once sliding starts, kinetic friction (μk < μs) takes over. The angle of repose corresponds to the verge of sliding, so limiting friction is correct.

items.question: Does the mass of the block affect the angle of repose?
items.answer: No. In the derivation: mg sin α = μs mg cos α. The mass m cancels on both sides, giving tan α = μs, which is independent of m. The angle of repose depends only on the materials of the block and surface, not on the block’s mass or weight.

items.question: Is the angle of repose always equal to the angle of friction?
items.answer: Yes, for a single body on a flat inclined surface (the standard NEET scenario): tan α = μs = tan θ, so α = θ. The NCERT explicitly states: ‘angle of repose = angle of friction’. This equality holds whenever the same coefficient of static friction governs both situations.

items.question: What happens if the slope angle exceeds the angle of repose?
items.answer: If slope > angle of repose, mg sin α > μs mg cos α. Static friction cannot balance the gravitational component along the slope. The block starts sliding. Once sliding, kinetic friction μk < μs applies, and the net force along the slope is mg sin α − μk mg cos α > 0, so the block accelerates.

grounding_summary: Page 238 defines angle of repose as the angle of the inclined plane with the horizontal such that a body placed on it just begins to slide.
The page derives tan α = μs from force balance on the inclined plane and states that coefficient of limiting friction equals tan of angle of repose.
The textbook explicitly states the equality: angle of repose = angle of friction (α = θ), with the relation μs = tan α = tan θ used to connect both concepts.
grounding_excerpt: Angle of repose is defined as the angle of the inclined plane with horizontal such that a body placed on it just begins to slide.
Thus the coefficient of limiting friction is equal to the tangent of angle of repose.
As well as $\alpha = \theta$ i.e. angle of repose = angle of friction.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Laws of Motion (Angle of Repose)
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source for angle of repose definition, derivation tan α = μs, and equality with angle of friction as tested in NEET.

sources.title: Friction — Physics Class 11 — NEET (EduRev)
sources.why_used: Confirms NEET exam patterns for angle of repose including definition MCQs and the α = θ equality as a direct test question.

review.notes: Content grounded in page-238 OCR markdown. Verbatim excerpts verified from source text. Derivation and equality follow textbook exactly. No dual_cta.
